problem: If I have a $4\times 4$ chess board, in how many ways can I place four distinct pawns on the board such that each column and row of the board contains no more than one pawn?

classified_questions.question: How many rows and columns are there on the chess board?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Why must there be exactly one pawn in each column and each row?
classified_questions.label: f

classified_questions.question: How many potential spots are there to place a pawn in the first row? Why?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How does the placement of a pawn in the first row affect the number of potential spots in the second row?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: Can you explain why there are only two potential spots in the third row?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: How many possible orderings of the pawns are there if they are all distinct?
classified_questions.label: b

classified_questions.question: What is the final answer to the problem?
classified_questions.label: b
